---
title: "Longleaf Pine (Pinus palustris)"
slug: "pinus-palustris"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/623578849/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Longleaf Pine"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Pinopsida"
tax_order: "Pinales"
tax_family: "Pinaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Pinopsida", "Pinales", "Pinaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/623578849/medium.jpg" alt="Longleaf Pine" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/hostetm">
hostetm</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342428394">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Pinopsida
-   **Order:** Pinales
-   **Family:** Pinaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=81901)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry to mesic sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A large conifer characterized by its very long needles, thick bark, and tall, straight trunk. In Florida, it is found in dry to mesic sandy habitats such as flatwoods, sandhills, and pine savannas. It grows year-round in warm conditions and is highly adapted to frequent fire, playing a foundational role in maintaining diverse, fire-dependent ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/81901-Pinus-palustris)
-   [Local profile](https://www.conifers.org/pi/Pinus_palustris.php)
